description: Explains how pytest is installed, how contributors prepare a local checkout, and how tox, pre-commit, documentation builds, changelog entries, and release tooling fit into the development workflow.
document:
pytest presents two setup paths: one for users installing the test runner and one for contributors working on the project itself. The public getting-started guide installs pytest with pip and immediately verifies the command-line entry point by asking for the version. The package metadata in `pyproject.toml` reinforces that pytest is a Python-only project requiring Python 3.10 or newer, with console scripts for both the modern command name and the older dotted spelling pointing at the same console entry. The README frames pytest as a small-test-friendly framework that scales to larger functional testing, and sends users to the documentation for installation, tutorials, and complete manuals.

The contributor workflow starts from GitHub rather than from a source archive. `CONTRIBUTING.rst` asks developers to fork the repository, clone their fork, fetch tags from the official repository when needed, and create a branch from main. The tag step matters because the project uses version information during local testing. Contributors are also expected to install pre-commit and enable its hook in the checkout so formatting and style checks run before commits. The guide still asks contributors to follow PEP 8 naming, but the normal enforcement path is automated through the hook and the tox linting environment.

The preferred local test runner is tox. The short contribution recipe recommends running linting plus a current Python test environment for ordinary changes, while the longer guide shows the generic linting-and-default-Python form and examples for passing pytest options or selecting one test module. The repository-level `tox.ini` is broader than that quick path: it covers supported CPython versions, PyPy, doctests, documentation, link checks, plugin integration, frozen-executable testing, and selected dependency combinations. That split lets contributors run a small useful subset locally while maintainers and CI keep named environments for wider coverage.

For developers who do not want tox for every edit-test loop, the contribution guide documents a direct editable install. It creates a virtual environment, activates it, installs the checkout with the development extra, and then runs pytest directly against selected tests. The package metadata defines that development extra with libraries for testing, requests, packaging, and schema validation. Tox also relies on the same extra for regular test environments, so the direct virtualenv workflow and the tox workflow share the same dependency intent even though tox isolates and builds environments automatically.

The tox configuration makes packaging part of normal testing. It requires tox 4, builds a wheel through a shared package environment, and passes the setuptools-scm pretend-version variable into that package step when needed. Regular test environments install the development extra, run pytest with positional arguments forwarded through environment variables, and add variant-specific behavior for coverage, doctests, parallel runs, open-file checks, and dependency checks. The linting environment skips installing pytest and runs pre-commit over all files. The docs environment uses the documentation requirements and Sphinx to build HTML under `doc/en/_build/html`, treating warnings as failures while keeping the build going enough to report multiple problems.

Development work is expected to include the bookkeeping that makes a pull request reviewable. Nontrivial behavior changes generally need a changelog file named from the issue number and change type, while trivial documentation-only fixes may skip it. Contributors normally add themselves to AUTHORS in alphabetical order unless the change is trivial or documentation-only. Tests for pytest itself often use the pytester fixture as a black-box harness that writes temporary test files, invokes pytest, and checks outcomes or terminal output. The development guide is intentionally short because it points to the contributing page for day-to-day development and to `RELEASING.rst` for release procedure.

Release setup is documented separately from ordinary contribution setup. The release policy aims for frequent bug-fix releases and minor releases every few months, using maintenance branches rather than releasing directly from main. The preferred preparation path is an automated GitHub workflow that chooses the version shape from release inputs and changelog contents, while the manual path must run on Linux and uses tox to generate release materials. Final publishing goes through a deploy workflow that publishes to PyPI and tags the repository, after which the release PR is merged without squashing so the tagged commit remains in the target branch. User support is documented through GitHub issues, Open Collective funding, and Tidelift coordination.
